metadata.description: 50 items testing selective attention in chemistry/chemical safety/chemical security contexts. Each item presents 3 information sources with conflicting chemical safety claims. The model must answer based ONLY on a designated source, ignoring plausible alternatives.

sources.label: A
sources.name: UNECE GHS Purple Book (Rev. 10, 2023)
sources.text: The Globally Harmonized System includes nine standardized hazard pictograms, each enclosed in a red diamond-shaped border on a white background.

sources.label: B
sources.name: European Chemicals Agency Technical Guide (2024)
sources.text: Under the CLP regulation, seven pictograms are used to communicate chemical hazards, as two GHS pictograms were not adopted into EU implementation.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Safety Board Training Manual (2023)
sources.text: The GHS classification system uses eleven pictograms covering physical, health, and environmental hazard categories.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (UNECE GHS Purple Book (Rev. 10, 2023)), how many GHS hazard pictograms exist?

items.topic: Flash point threshold for GHS flammable liquid Category 1

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Hazard Communication Guide (2024)
sources.text: GHS Category 1 flammable liquids have a flash point below 23 degrees Celsius and a boiling point at or below 35 degrees Celsius.

sources.label: B
sources.name: NFPA Fire Protection Handbook (2023)
sources.text: The most hazardous flammable liquid classification applies to liquids with flash points below 37.8 degrees Celsius (100°F), consistent with the Class I designation.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Transport Canada TDG Reference (2024)
sources.text: Flammable liquids in the highest hazard category have flash points below 0 degrees Celsius, reflecting their extreme volatility even in cold conditions.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (OSHA Hazard Communication Guide (2024)), what is the flash point threshold for GHS Category 1 flammable liquids?

sources.label: A
sources.name: NFPA 704 Standard (2022 Edition)
sources.text: The blue quadrant of the NFPA 704 diamond indicates health hazards, with ratings from 0 (no hazard) to 4 (deadly).

sources.label: B
sources.name: Emergency Response Guidebook (2024)
sources.text: In the NFPA diamond system, the blue section represents reactivity hazards, alerting responders to chemicals that may undergo violent reactions.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Plant Safety Manual (2023)
sources.text: Blue in the NFPA 704 system indicates water reactivity hazards, warning that the substance reacts dangerously with water.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (Emergency Response Guidebook (2024)), what does the blue section of the NFPA 704 diamond represent?

items.topic: GHS acute toxicity Category 4 oral LD50 range

sources.label: A
sources.name: UNECE GHS Chapter 3.1 (2023)
sources.text: Acute toxicity Category 4 for the oral route covers substances with LD50 values greater than 300 mg/kg but not more than 2000 mg/kg.

sources.label: B
sources.name: EPA Pesticide Registration Manual (2024)
sources.text: Category IV acute oral toxicity applies to substances with LD50 values above 5000 mg/kg, representing the lowest toxicity classification.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Industrial Toxicology Reference (2023)
sources.text: The fourth category of acute oral toxicity encompasses LD50 values from 500 to 5000 mg/kg based on standard rat oral studies.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (Industrial Toxicology Reference (2023)), what is the LD50 range for Category 4 acute oral toxicity?

items.topic: Number of sections in GHS-compliant SDS

sources.label: A
sources.name: ISO 11014 Standard (2024)
sources.text: A GHS-compliant Safety Data Sheet contains 16 standardized sections, covering identification through regulatory and other information.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Manufacturers Association Guide (2023)
sources.text: The modern Safety Data Sheet format consists of 12 sections, covering the essential hazard information that workers need for safe handling.

sources.label: C
sources.name: EU REACH Guidance on SDS (2024)
sources.text: An SDS under REACH contains 16 sections plus an annex for exposure scenarios, making it effectively a 17-section document for registered substances.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (EU REACH Guidance on SDS (2024)), how many sections does an SDS under REACH effectively contain?

items.topic: GHS signal word for most severe hazards

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Quick Reference Card (2024)
sources.text: The GHS signal word 'Danger' is assigned to the more severe hazard categories, indicating immediate or serious hazard potential.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Canadian WHMIS Training Guide (2023)
sources.text: Under WHMIS 2015, the signal word 'Warning' is used for the most critical chemical hazards requiring immediate attention and control.

sources.label: C
sources.name: ANSI Z400.1 Reference (2023)
sources.text: The highest-severity GHS signal word is 'Caution,' reserved for Category 1 hazards across all physical, health, and environmental classes.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (OSHA Quick Reference Card (2024)), what GHS signal word is assigned to the most severe hazard categories?

sources.label: A
sources.name: GHS Implementation Guide (2024)
sources.text: Physical hazard H-statements use codes in the H200-H299 range, covering explosives, flammable gases, aerosols, and other physical hazard classes.

sources.label: B
sources.name: European CLP Guide (2023)
sources.text: Physical hazard statements under CLP are coded H100-H199, following the EU numbering convention for physical property-related hazards.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Japan GHS Classification Manual (2024)
sources.text: Physical hazard codes run from H400 to H499 in the Japanese implementation of GHS, which uses a reverse numbering system compared to the UN standard.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (GHS Implementation Guide (2024)), what H-statement code range covers physical hazards?

items.topic: Minimum label element required by GHS

sources.label: A
sources.name: UNECE GHS Labeling Guide (2024)
sources.text: GHS labels must include six elements: product identifier, signal word, hazard statement(s), precautionary statement(s), pictogram(s), and supplier identification.

sources.label: B
sources.name: OSHA HazCom Fact Sheet (2023)
sources.text: Required GHS label elements include the product name, pictogram, signal word, and hazard statements -- a total of four mandatory components.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Safe Work Australia Guide (2024)
sources.text: Australian WHS labeling requires eight elements including a product identifier, GHS pictograms, signal word, hazard statements, precautionary statements, supplier details, expiry date, and first aid information.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (UNECE GHS Labeling Guide (2024)), how many elements must a GHS label include?

items.topic: Boiling point threshold for flammable gas classification

sources.label: A
sources.name: GHS Classification Criteria (2024)
sources.text: A flammable gas is defined as a gas having a flammable range with air at 20 degrees Celsius and a standard pressure of 101.3 kPa.

sources.label: B
sources.name: DOT Hazmat Guide (2023)
sources.text: Flammable gases are those with a boiling point of 20 degrees Celsius or below at atmospheric pressure, regardless of their flammability range.

sources.label: C
sources.name: NFPA Gas Classification Manual (2024)
sources.text: Gases are classified as flammable when their lower explosive limit is 5 percent or less by volume in air at 25 degrees Celsius.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (DOT Hazmat Guide (2023)), what defines a flammable gas?
items.gold_answer: Boiling point of 20 degrees Celsius or below
items.answer_aliases: boiling point of 20 degrees Celsius or below|boiling point 20°C or below|20°C boiling point

sources.label: A
sources.name: GHS Pictogram Reference (2024)
sources.text: The environment pictogram showing a dead fish and tree is coded GHS09 and is used for substances hazardous to the aquatic environment.

sources.label: B
sources.name: EU CLP Pictogram Guide (2023)
sources.text: Environmental hazards are represented by pictogram GHS07, the exclamation mark, which alerts users to ecotoxicity concerns alongside its health hazard indication.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Transport Safety Council Guide (2024)
sources.text: The environmental hazard pictogram is designated GHS10 in the expanded GHS framework that includes separate symbols for aquatic, terrestrial, and atmospheric hazards.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (GHS Pictogram Reference (2024)), what is the code for the environment pictogram?

sources.label: A
sources.name: ANSI Z358.1 Standard (2024)
sources.text: Emergency eyewash equipment shall be designed to deliver flushing fluid to the eyes for a minimum of 15 minutes.

sources.label: B
sources.name: UK HSE First Aid Guide (2023)
sources.text: For chemical eye injuries, irrigate the affected eye with clean water for a minimum of 20 minutes while awaiting emergency medical services.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Laboratory Safety Handbook (2024)
sources.text: Flush eyes for at least 10 minutes following chemical splash exposure, ensuring water flows from the inner corner outward.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (UK HSE First Aid Guide (2023)), how long should eyes be irrigated after chemical exposure?

items.topic: Chemical fume hood face velocity standard

sources.label: A
sources.name: ANSI/AIHA Z9.5 Standard (2024)
sources.text: Laboratory fume hoods should maintain an average face velocity of 80 to 120 feet per minute at the sash operating position.

sources.label: B
sources.name: OSHA Technical Manual (2023)
sources.text: A minimum face velocity of 100 feet per minute is recommended for chemical fume hoods to provide adequate containment of hazardous vapors.

sources.label: C
sources.name: European Standard EN 14175 (2024)
sources.text: Fume cupboard containment testing requires a face velocity of at least 0.5 meters per second, equivalent to approximately 98 feet per minute.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (European Standard EN 14175 (2024)), what face velocity is required for fume cupboard testing?
items.answer_aliases: 0.5 meters per second|0.5 m/s|98 fpm|0.5 m/s (98 fpm)

items.topic: Fire extinguisher class for electrical fires

sources.label: A
sources.name: NFPA 10 Standard (2024)
sources.text: Class C fire extinguishers are rated for fires involving energized electrical equipment. The C rating indicates the extinguishing agent is non-conductive.

sources.label: B
sources.name: British Fire Safety Guide (2023)
sources.text: Electrical fires are designated as Class E in the British and European fire classification system, requiring CO2 or dry powder extinguishers.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Australian Fire Standards (2024)
sources.text: Fires involving electrical equipment are classified as Class F under Australian standards, with the F designation standing for 'Fuse-related' hazards.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (NFPA 10 Standard (2024)), what class of fire extinguisher is rated for electrical fires?

sources.label: A
sources.name: NSF/ANSI 49 Standard (2024)
sources.text: Biological safety cabinets must be field-certified at least annually and whenever the unit is moved, repaired, or HEPA filters are replaced.

sources.label: B
sources.name: CDC BMBL Guide (2023)
sources.text: BSC performance verification should be conducted every six months in high-containment facilities operating at BSL-3 or BSL-4.

sources.label: C
sources.name: WHO Laboratory Biosafety Manual (2024)
sources.text: Biosafety cabinets require certification every two years under the WHO's recommended maintenance schedule for resource-limited settings.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (CDC BMBL Guide (2023)), how often should BSC performance be verified in high-containment facilities?

items.topic: Maximum travel distance to safety shower

sources.label: A
sources.name: ANSI Z358.1 Standard (2024)
sources.text: Emergency safety showers shall be located so that they can be reached within 10 seconds of travel time from the hazard.

sources.label: B
sources.name: European Safety Standards Guide (2023)
sources.text: Safety showers must be positioned no more than 6 meters from any workstation where corrosive or irritating chemicals are used.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Process Safety Manual (2024)
sources.text: Emergency showers should be accessible within 25 feet of any location where hazardous chemicals are handled.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (ANSI Z358.1 Standard (2024)), how quickly must a safety shower be reachable?

sources.label: A
sources.name: CDC Sterilization Guidelines (2024)
sources.text: Geobacillus stearothermophilus spores are the standard biological indicator for validating steam autoclave sterilization cycles at 121 degrees Celsius.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Pharmaceutical Sterilization Manual (2023)
sources.text: Bacillus atrophaeus spores are used as the standard biological indicator for autoclave validation, as their heat resistance closely matches the target sterilization conditions.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Laboratory Equipment Guide (2024)
sources.text: Bacillus pumilus is the recommended biological indicator organism for steam sterilization validation in clinical and research laboratories.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (CDC Sterilization Guidelines (2024)), what organism is used as the biological indicator for steam autoclave validation?

sources.label: A
sources.name: CDC PPE Doffing Protocol (2024)
sources.text: The recommended doffing sequence begins with glove removal, as gloves are the most contaminated piece of PPE and removing them first minimizes cross-contamination risk.

sources.label: B
sources.name: European Biosafety Network Guide (2023)
sources.text: PPE removal should begin with the gown or coverall, which can be peeled off along with the outer gloves in a single step to minimize contamination.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Industrial Hygiene Handbook (2024)
sources.text: Eye and face protection should be removed first during PPE doffing because the face shield or goggles are the outermost layer and least likely to be directly contaminated.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (CDC PPE Doffing Protocol (2024)), what PPE should be removed first?

sources.label: A
sources.name: NFPA 30 Standard (2024)
sources.text: An approved flammable liquid storage cabinet may hold a maximum of 60 gallons of Class I or Class II flammable liquids.

sources.label: B
sources.name: OSHA Lab Safety Guide (2023)
sources.text: Standard flammable storage cabinets are designed for a maximum capacity of 45 gallons, with double-walled steel construction and self-closing doors.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Safety Board Recommendations (2024)
sources.text: Flammable liquid storage cabinets should not exceed 30 gallons total capacity to maintain structural integrity during a fire event.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (NFPA 30 Standard (2024)), what is the maximum capacity of a flammable liquid storage cabinet?

items.topic: Chemical spill kit neutralizing agent for acid spills

sources.label: A
sources.name: Laboratory Spill Response Manual (2024)
sources.text: Sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) is the standard neutralizing agent for acid spills in laboratory settings, converting acids to neutral sodium salts.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Emergency Response Guide (2023)
sources.text: Calcium hydroxide (slaked lime) is the preferred neutralizer for acid spills because of its high buffering capacity and availability in bulk quantities.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Industrial Safety Procedures (2024)
sources.text: Sodium hydroxide solution is recommended for neutralizing acid spills, as its strong alkalinity rapidly brings the pH to neutral.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (Chemical Emergency Response Guide (2023)), what neutralizing agent is preferred for acid spills?

items.topic: OSHA eyewash station flow rate requirement

sources.label: A
sources.name: ANSI Z358.1 Standard (2024)
sources.text: Plumbed emergency eyewash units shall provide a controlled flow of flushing fluid at a minimum of 0.4 gallons per minute (1.5 liters per minute) for 15 minutes.

sources.label: B
sources.name: European EN 15154 Standard (2023)
sources.text: Eye irrigation equipment must deliver a minimum flow rate of 6 liters per minute for at least 15 minutes to ensure adequate flushing.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Workplace Safety Manual (2024)
sources.text: Eyewash stations require a flow rate of at least 3 gallons per minute to provide sufficient flushing pressure and volume for effective decontamination.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (Workplace Safety Manual (2024)), what flow rate is required for eyewash stations?

items.topic: OSHA PEL for formaldehyde as 8-hour TWA

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Formaldehyde Standard 1910.1048 (2024)
sources.text: The OSHA permissible exposure limit for formaldehyde is 0.75 parts per million as an 8-hour time-weighted average.

sources.label: B
sources.name: ACGIH TLV Documentation (2024)
sources.text: The Threshold Limit Value for formaldehyde has been set at 0.1 ppm as a ceiling concentration that should not be exceeded at any time.

sources.label: C
sources.name: NIOSH Pocket Guide (2023)
sources.text: NIOSH recommends an exposure limit of 0.016 ppm for formaldehyde as a 10-hour TWA based on its classification as a potential occupational carcinogen.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (OSHA Formaldehyde Standard 1910.1048 (2024)), what is the PEL for formaldehyde?

sources.label: A
sources.name: ACGIH TLV Booklet (2024)
sources.text: The TLV-STEL is defined as a 15-minute time-weighted average exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during a workday.

sources.label: B
sources.name: OSHA Industrial Hygiene Guide (2023)
sources.text: Short-term exposure limits are measured over a 30-minute averaging period to capture peak concentrations during process upsets and batch operations.

sources.label: C
sources.name: European OEL Directive (2024)
sources.text: Short-term exposure values apply to a reference period of 10 minutes, representing the maximum allowable concentration for brief peak exposures.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (ACGIH TLV Booklet (2024)), how long is a STEL measurement period?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Benzene Standard 1910.1028 (2024)
sources.text: The permissible exposure limit for benzene is 1 part per million as an 8-hour time-weighted average.

sources.label: B
sources.name: ACGIH TLV List (2024)
sources.text: The TLV-TWA for benzene is 0.5 ppm, with a skin notation indicating that dermal absorption contributes significantly to overall exposure.

sources.label: C
sources.name: NIOSH Criteria Document (2023)
sources.text: NIOSH recommends a REL of 0.1 ppm for benzene as a 10-hour TWA to minimize leukemia risk among chronically exposed workers.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (NIOSH Criteria Document (2023)), what is the recommended exposure limit for benzene?

items.topic: Route of exposure for LC50 measurement

sources.label: A
sources.name: OECD Test Guideline 403 (2024)
sources.text: LC50 values measure the concentration of an airborne substance that is lethal to 50 percent of test animals following a 4-hour inhalation exposure.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Toxicology Textbook (2023)
sources.text: LC50 measures the lethal concentration through dermal absorption, determined by applying the substance to a defined skin area for a specified duration.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Regulatory Toxicology Reference (2024)
sources.text: LC50 is the lethal concentration measured through oral administration dissolved in water, reflecting the aquatic toxicity of the substance.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (OECD Test Guideline 403 (2024)), what route of exposure does LC50 measure?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA General Industry Standards (2024)
sources.text: The OSHA ceiling limit for hydrogen sulfide is 20 ppm, with a peak concentration of 50 ppm for a maximum of 10 minutes if no other measurable exposure occurs.

sources.label: B
sources.name: ACGIH TLV Documentation (2024)
sources.text: The TLV-TWA for hydrogen sulfide is 1 ppm with a STEL of 5 ppm, reflecting the substance's strong olfactory fatigue at low concentrations.

sources.label: C
sources.name: NIOSH Pocket Guide (2023)
sources.text: NIOSH recommends a 10-minute ceiling of 10 ppm for hydrogen sulfide to prevent olfactory fatigue and acute overexposure.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (ACGIH TLV Documentation (2024)), what is the TLV-TWA for hydrogen sulfide?

sources.label: A
sources.name: NIOSH IDLH Documentation (2024)
sources.text: The IDLH concentration for ammonia is 300 ppm, based on acute inhalation toxicity data and the need for worker escape without respiratory protection.

sources.label: B
sources.name: OSHA Ammonia Safety Guide (2023)
sources.text: Ammonia becomes immediately dangerous to life at concentrations above 500 ppm, where severe respiratory damage occurs within minutes of exposure.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Emergency Response Manual (2024)
sources.text: The IDLH value for anhydrous ammonia is 150 ppm, which was revised downward to account for individual variability in ammonia sensitivity.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (NIOSH IDLH Documentation (2024)), what is the IDLH concentration for ammonia?

items.topic: Meaning of skin notation on TLV

sources.label: A
sources.name: ACGIH TLV Documentation (2024)
sources.text: The 'Skin' notation indicates that the substance can be absorbed through intact skin in sufficient quantities to contribute to overall systemic exposure.
sources.claim_value: dermal absorption contributes to systemic exposure

sources.label: B
sources.name: Occupational Health Guide (2023)
sources.text: The skin notation warns that the substance is a skin sensitizer that may cause allergic contact dermatitis upon repeated dermal exposure.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Industrial Hygiene Reference (2024)
sources.text: A skin notation on the TLV signifies that the substance causes direct corrosive damage to the skin and requires impervious glove protection.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (ACGIH TLV Documentation (2024)), what does the 'Skin' notation indicate?
items.gold_answer: Dermal absorption contributes to systemic exposure
items.answer_aliases: dermal absorption|absorbed through skin|skin absorption|absorbed through intact skin|dermal absorption contributes to systemic exposure

items.topic: Number of STEL excursions allowed per day

sources.label: A
sources.name: ACGIH TLV Guidelines (2024)
sources.text: No more than four 15-minute STEL excursions per day are recommended, with at least 60 minutes between successive excursions.

sources.label: B
sources.name: OSHA Compliance Guide (2023)
sources.text: Up to six STEL excursions are permitted within an 8-hour shift, provided the cumulative exposure time does not exceed the TWA.

sources.label: C
sources.name: European OEL Framework (2024)
sources.text: A maximum of two short-term excursions per shift is allowed under EU occupational exposure standards.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (ACGIH TLV Guidelines (2024)), how many STEL excursions per day are recommended?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Lead Standards 1910.1025 (2024)
sources.text: The permissible exposure limit for lead in general industry is 50 micrograms per cubic meter of air as an 8-hour TWA.

sources.label: B
sources.name: ACGIH TLV List (2024)
sources.text: The TLV-TWA for inorganic lead is 0.05 mg/m³ with an A3 confirmed animal carcinogen designation.

sources.label: C
sources.name: NIOSH Criteria for Lead (2023)
sources.text: NIOSH recommends an airborne REL of 0.05 mg/m³ for lead, with blood lead monitoring triggered at half this concentration.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (OSHA Lead Standards 1910.1025 (2024)), what is the PEL for lead?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Industrial Hygiene Manual (2024)
sources.text: The action level is typically set at one-half of the PEL and triggers requirements for exposure monitoring, medical surveillance, and employee notification.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Safety Compliance Guide (2023)
sources.text: The action level is defined as one-quarter of the permissible exposure limit, representing the threshold at which employers must initiate a monitoring program.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Occupational Medicine Reference (2024)
sources.text: OSHA action levels are set at 75 percent of the PEL, providing a margin of safety before the legally enforceable limit is reached.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (OSHA Industrial Hygiene Manual (2024)), what fraction of the PEL defines the action level?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OPCW Implementation Guide (2024)
sources.text: Schedule 2 chemicals are dual-use substances produced in relatively small commercial quantities but which could serve as precursors to chemical weapons or have direct toxic properties.
sources.claim_value: dual-use, small commercial quantities, precursor potential

sources.label: B
sources.name: Arms Control Textbook (2023)
sources.text: CWC Schedule 2 covers chemicals with widespread industrial applications produced in large volumes, which are subject only to end-use monitoring rather than quantity restrictions.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Defense Reference (2024)
sources.text: Schedule 2 includes chemicals with no legitimate commercial use that are exclusively associated with weapons production programs.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (OPCW Implementation Guide (2024)), what characterizes CWC Schedule 2 chemicals?
items.gold_answer: Dual-use substances produced in relatively small commercial quantities
items.answer_aliases: dual-use|small commercial quantities|dual-use substances|precursors|dual-use, small commercial quantities

sources.label: A
sources.name: ECHA REACH Guide (2024)
sources.text: Substances manufactured or imported into the EU in quantities of one tonne or more per year per registrant must be registered with ECHA.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Industry Association (2023)
sources.text: REACH registration is required for substances exceeding 10 tonnes per year, with lower tonnage bands subject only to notification requirements.

sources.label: C
sources.name: EU Trade Compliance Manual (2024)
sources.text: Chemical substances must be registered under REACH when imported in quantities exceeding 100 kilograms per year per company.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (ECHA REACH Guide (2024)), what is the minimum tonnage requiring REACH registration?
items.answer_aliases: one tonne per year|1 tonne per year|1 tonne|one tonne

sources.label: A
sources.name: 49 CFR Part 173 (2024)
sources.text: The DOT hazardous materials regulations define nine primary hazard classes for the classification and transport of dangerous goods.

sources.label: B
sources.name: International Shipping Guide (2023)
sources.text: There are seven hazard classes under the UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods, excluding subsidiary risk categories.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Freight Safety Manual (2024)
sources.text: DOT recognizes twelve hazard classes that comprehensively cover all categories of dangerous materials shipped by road, rail, air, and water.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (49 CFR Part 173 (2024)), how many primary DOT hazard classes exist?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OPCW Historical Records (2024)
sources.text: The Chemical Weapons Convention entered into force on April 29, 1997, following its adoption by the United Nations Conference on Disarmament in 1992.

sources.label: B
sources.name: International Law Reference (2023)
sources.text: The CWC became legally binding on January 13, 1993, the date it was opened for signature at the Paris signing ceremony.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Arms Control Chronology (2024)
sources.text: The Chemical Weapons Convention entered into force in 2001 after achieving the required number of ratifications from signatory states.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (OPCW Historical Records (2024)), when did the CWC enter into force?

items.topic: TSCA Chemical Substance Inventory update frequency

sources.label: A
sources.name: EPA TSCA Implementation Guide (2024)
sources.text: The EPA conducts a comprehensive reset of the TSCA Inventory every 10 years under the Lautenberg Chemical Safety Act amendments, requiring active manufacturers and importers to notify EPA of their substances.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Regulatory Guide (2023)
sources.text: The TSCA Inventory is updated continuously as new Pre-Manufacture Notifications are approved, with no fixed reset cycle.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Industry Compliance Manual (2024)
sources.text: EPA resets the TSCA Chemical Substance Inventory annually, requiring all registrants to reconfirm their active substance listings each calendar year.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (Chemical Regulatory Guide (2023)), how is the TSCA Inventory updated?

sources.label: A
sources.name: UNECE GHS Sub-Committee (2024)
sources.text: The GHS is revised on a two-year cycle, with each revision incorporating updated classification criteria, guidance, and new hazard categories as needed.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Regulation Review (2023)
sources.text: Major GHS revisions occur every five years, with minor amendments published annually as addenda to the existing revision.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Regulatory Harmonization Guide (2024)
sources.text: GHS updates are published on a three-year cycle, synchronized with the UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods revision schedule.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (UNECE GHS Sub-Committee (2024)), how often is the GHS revised?

items.topic: Number of states parties to the CWC

sources.label: A
sources.name: OPCW Annual Report (2024)
sources.text: As of 2024, the Chemical Weapons Convention has 193 states parties, making it one of the most widely adopted disarmament treaties.

sources.label: B
sources.name: UN Disarmament Yearbook (2023)
sources.text: The CWC has been ratified by 180 countries, with the remaining UN member states either having signed but not ratified or not signed at all.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Arms Control Association (2024)
sources.text: A total of 197 countries are party to the Chemical Weapons Convention, achieving near-universal adherence to the treaty.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (OPCW Annual Report (2024)), how many states parties does the CWC have?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OSHA Recordkeeping Standard (2024)
sources.text: Employers must record work-related injuries and illnesses on OSHA Form 300, the Log of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Safety Management Handbook (2023)
sources.text: Work-related injuries are documented on OSHA Form 301, the Injury and Illness Incident Report, which captures detailed information about each case.

sources.label: C
sources.name: HR Compliance Guide (2024)
sources.text: Employers use OSHA Form 200 to maintain a running log of all workplace injuries and illnesses throughout the calendar year.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (HR Compliance Guide (2024)), what OSHA form is used to log workplace injuries?

sources.label: A
sources.name: ECHA About Page (2024)
sources.text: The European Chemicals Agency is headquartered in Helsinki, Finland, where it manages the REACH, CLP, and Biocidal Products regulations.

sources.label: B
sources.name: EU Agency Directory (2023)
sources.text: ECHA is based in Brussels, Belgium, alongside the European Commission and other EU regulatory bodies.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Industry Guide (2024)
sources.text: The European Chemicals Agency operates from its headquarters in The Hague, Netherlands, near the International Court of Justice.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (ECHA About Page (2024)), where is ECHA headquartered?

sources.label: A
sources.name: EPA Risk Management Program (2024)
sources.text: Facilities that store, process, or handle chlorine in quantities at or above 2,500 pounds are subject to EPA's Risk Management Program requirements.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Safety Guide (2023)
sources.text: The RMP threshold for chlorine is 10,000 pounds, consistent with the general threshold quantity for toxic substances under Section 112(r) of the Clean Air Act.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Water Treatment Safety Manual (2024)
sources.text: Chlorine triggers RMP requirements at quantities above 500 pounds, reflecting its high acute toxicity and widespread use in water treatment facilities.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (EPA Risk Management Program (2024)), what is the RMP threshold quantity for chlorine?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OPCW Schedule Reference (2024)
sources.text: Schedule 1 chemicals include nerve agents such as sarin (GB), soman (GD), tabun (GA), and VX, as well as sulfur mustard (HD) and nitrogen mustards.
sources.claim_value: sarin, soman, tabun, VX, sulfur mustard

sources.label: B
sources.name: Chemical Defense Manual (2023)
sources.text: Schedule 1 lists primarily include industrial chemicals with weapons potential such as phosgene (CG), chlorine, and hydrogen cyanide (AC).

sources.label: C

sources.text: CWC Schedule 1 consists of riot control agents including CS gas, CR gas, and OC (pepper spray) that are banned for use in warfare.

sources.label: A
sources.name: DHS CFATS Program Guide (2024)
sources.text: The Chemical Facility Anti-Terrorism Standards program is administered by the Department of Homeland Security's Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA).

sources.label: B

sources.text: CFATS is implemented by the Environmental Protection Agency as part of its broader chemical facility safety oversight responsibilities under the Clean Air Act.

sources.label: C

sources.text: The Department of Defense administers CFATS for chemical facilities deemed critical to national defense supply chains.

items.topic: Number of Australia Group member countries

sources.label: A
sources.name: Australia Group Website (2024)
sources.text: The Australia Group currently has 43 participants including the European Union, coordinating export controls on chemical and biological weapons precursors.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Nonproliferation Review (2023)
sources.text: Sixty-two countries participate in the Australia Group, which has expanded significantly since its establishment in 1985 to include most OECD members.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Export Control Reference (2024)
sources.text: The Australia Group consists of 28 member states, primarily Western nations that share common export control standards for dual-use chemicals.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source A (Australia Group Website (2024)), how many participants does the Australia Group have?

sources.label: A
sources.name: Medical Countermeasures Guide (2024)
sources.text: Organophosphate nerve agents exert their toxic effects by irreversibly inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, causing accumulation of acetylcholine at nerve synapses.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Emergency Medicine Reference (2023)
sources.text: Nerve agents primarily act by blocking sodium channels in nerve cell membranes, preventing the propagation of action potentials.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Toxicology Handbook (2024)
sources.text: The primary mechanism of nerve agent toxicity is competitive antagonism of GABA receptors in the central nervous system, causing uncontrolled neural excitation.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (Medical Countermeasures Guide (2024)), how do nerve agents exert their toxic effects?

items.topic: Antidote for organophosphate nerve agent exposure

sources.label: A
sources.name: US Army Medical Field Manual (2024)
sources.text: Atropine sulfate is the primary antidote for nerve agent exposure, blocking the effects of excess acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Civilian Emergency Medicine Guide (2023)
sources.text: Naloxone is the first-line antidote for nerve agent poisoning, reversing the respiratory depression that is the primary cause of death.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Hazmat Response Protocol (2024)
sources.text: Calcium gluconate is the standard antidote administered following nerve agent exposure to stabilize cardiac function and prevent arrhythmias.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (US Army Medical Field Manual (2024)), what is the primary antidote for nerve agent exposure?

sources.label: A
sources.name: OPCW Official Website (2024)
sources.text: The Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons is headquartered in The Hague, Netherlands.

sources.label: B
sources.name: International Organizations Guide (2023)
sources.text: The OPCW maintains its headquarters in Geneva, Switzerland, alongside other international disarmament organizations.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Diplomatic Institutions Directory (2024)
sources.text: OPCW is based in Vienna, Austria, sharing facilities with the International Atomic Energy Agency and the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (International Organizations Guide (2023)), where is the OPCW headquartered?

items.topic: Classification of chlorine as CWA type

sources.label: A
sources.name: Chemical Defense Field Manual (2024)
sources.text: Chlorine is classified as a choking agent (pulmonary agent) that causes damage to the respiratory tract through direct chemical injury to lung tissue.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Toxicology Reference (2023)
sources.text: Chlorine is categorized as a blood agent because it enters the bloodstream through the lungs and disrupts cellular oxygen transport.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Emergency Response Manual (2024)
sources.text: Chlorine is designated as a vesicant (blister agent) due to the severe chemical burns it causes to skin, eyes, and respiratory mucous membranes.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (Chemical Defense Field Manual (2024)), how is chlorine classified as a chemical warfare agent?

items.topic: Year the Australia Group was established

sources.label: A
sources.name: Australia Group History (2024)
sources.text: The Australia Group was established in 1985 in response to the use of chemical weapons during the Iran-Iraq War.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Export Control History (2023)
sources.text: The Australia Group was founded in 1992 as part of the international community's effort to complement the Chemical Weapons Convention with practical export controls.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Nonproliferation Timeline (2024)
sources.text: The informal export control arrangement known as the Australia Group began operations in 1978 following the Biological Weapons Convention review conference.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source C (Nonproliferation Timeline (2024)), when did the Australia Group begin operations?

items.topic: NATO designation for VX nerve agent

sources.label: A
sources.name: NATO CBRN Reference (2024)
sources.text: VX is the NATO designation for O-ethyl S-(2-diisopropylaminoethyl) methylphosphonothioate, a persistent V-series nerve agent.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Military Chemistry Manual (2023)
sources.text: The NATO code GA designates VX nerve agent, with the G indicating it belongs to the German-developed series of organophosphate warfare agents.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Chemical Warfare Dictionary (2024)
sources.text: VX is designated by the NATO code CX, categorizing it alongside other persistent blister agents in the military classification system.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (NATO CBRN Reference (2024)), what is the NATO designation for O-ethyl S-(2-diisopropylaminoethyl) methylphosphonothioate?

items.topic: Primary decontamination method for CW agents on equipment

sources.label: A
sources.name: NATO CBRN Decontamination Guide (2024)
sources.text: The primary method for decontaminating equipment exposed to chemical warfare agents is application of a reactive decontaminant solution such as DS2 (decontaminating solution 2) or sodium hydroxide-based solutions.

sources.label: B
sources.name: Emergency Response Protocol (2023)
sources.text: Steam cleaning at temperatures above 160 degrees Celsius is the standard method for decontaminating equipment following chemical agent exposure.

sources.label: C
sources.name: Civil Defense Handbook (2024)
sources.text: Ultraviolet light exposure for a minimum of 24 hours is the recommended equipment decontamination method, as UV radiation breaks down organophosphate compounds into non-toxic fragments.

items.question: Based ONLY on Source B (Emergency Response Protocol (2023)), what is the standard equipment decontamination method for CW agents?
